records.title: Creating the bot
records.content:
1. Go [here](https://discordapp.com/developers/applications/) and login.

2. Once on the dashboard click the "New Application" button.

3. Name the application whatever you want.

4. In the app dashboard press the menu button and select "Bot"

5. Press "Add Bot".

6. Now you can copy your token and change other aspects of the bot account.

7. IMPORTANT scroll and enable Server Members Intent on your bot. If you don't your bot may not function properly or host at all in the app.
records.searchableText:
Creating the bot
Starting out with the bot
1. Go [here](https://discordapp.com/developers/applications/) and login.

2. Once on the dashboard click the "New Application" button.

3. Name the application whatever you want.

4. In the app dashboard press the menu button and select "Bot"

5. Press "Add Bot".

6. Now you can copy your token and change other aspects of the bot account.

7. IMPORTANT scroll and enable Server Members Intent on your bot. If you don't your bot may not function properly or host at all in the app.

records.title: Inviting the bot
records.content:
Inviting the bot is also done from [here!](https://discordapp.com/developers/applications/)

Click on the OAuth2 button from the menu

In scopes check "bot"

Scroll down and select your bot permissions, "Administrator" counts for all permissions if you need them all."

Scroll back up to the scopes and copy then open the link. Note: You must have the "Manage Server" permission in the server you wish to add the bot to.

records.title: Cooldown fields
records.content:
## Cooldown fields

`cooldown?: number` is the duration in seconds. Zero or an omitted value disables the cooldown.

`cooldownType?: "User" | "Server" | "Global"` selects whether usage is tracked per user, per server, or across every server.

`cooldownMessage?: string` is an optional BCFD template used when blocked. It can include `$cooldownRemaining()`. Without a custom message, the app sends its standard remaining-time response.
records.searchableText:
Cooldown fields
Commands
Modern Command Fields
## Cooldown fields

`cooldown?: number` is the duration in seconds. Zero or an omitted value disables the cooldown.

`cooldownType?: "User" | "Server" | "Global"` selects whether usage is tracked per user, per server, or across every server.

`cooldownMessage?: string` is an optional BCFD template used when blocked. It can include `$cooldownRemaining()`. Without a custom message, the app sends its standard remaining-time response.

records.title: Reply and typing fields
records.content:
## Reply and typing fields

`channelMessageAsReply?: boolean` and `channelEmbedAsReply?: boolean` send the corresponding response as a Discord reply when a triggering message exists.

`channelMessageTyping?: boolean` and `channelEmbedTyping?: boolean` keep the typing indicator active while that response is being prepared.

These fields apply to channel responses. Event commands without a triggering message fall back to a normal channel send.

`channelMessageAsReply?: boolean` and `channelEmbedAsReply?: boolean` send the corresponding response as a Discord reply when a triggering message exists.

`channelMessageTyping?: boolean` and `channelEmbedTyping?: boolean` keep the typing indicator active while that response is being prepared.

These fields apply to channel responses. Event commands without a triggering message fall back to a normal channel send.

records.title: channelWhitelist and serverWhitelist
records.content:
## channelWhitelist and serverWhitelist

`channelWhitelist?: string` and `serverWhitelist?: string` contain comma-separated Discord IDs. When nonempty, the command runs only in a listed channel or server.

Use numeric Discord IDs, not names or mentions. Enable Developer Mode in Discord to copy IDs.

records.title: startsWith and phrase
records.content:
## startsWith and phrase

`startsWith: true` matches when a message begins with the command and allows arguments after it. `phrase: true` matches the command text anywhere in a message. With both false, the message must equal the command.

Use `$args(index)`, `$argsCount`, or `$messageAfterCommand` with Starts With commands.

records.title: Template context and failure behavior
records.content:
## Template context and failure behavior

User values require a caller, member values require server membership, mentioned values require a mention, and server or channel values require the corresponding Discord context. A value normally becomes empty when its context is unavailable.

Channel management requires Manage Channels. Role assignment requires Manage Roles and the bot's role must be above the target role. Kick, ban, and voice mute require their matching Discord permissions and valid targets.

Functions that reject invalid arguments or Discord operations return text beginning with `[BCFD Error:`. Lint templates before saving, but also account for runtime permission and context failures.

The current interpreter uses exact, case-sensitive keyword names. The optional legacy interpreter can have older spelling and behavior; check the app's interpreter setting when maintaining older commands.

records.title: What interactions are
records.content:
## What interactions are

Interactions are Discord slash commands. A user types `/`, chooses your command, fills in any options, and Discord sends the interaction to Bot Commander. The bot must be hosted for the command and its buttons to respond.

For a guided example, see the [Slash Commands](tutorial.html) and [Interactive Buttons](tutorial.html) tutorials.

records.title: Create and publish an interaction
records.content:
## Create and publish an interaction

- Open the Interactions tab and create a new interaction.

- Enter its command name, description, options, and root response action.

- Save the interaction and host the bot.

- Register the interaction, or use Sync All to make Discord match the app's interaction list.

- Run the slash command in Discord and verify its response.

records.title: Registration and propagation
records.content:
## Registration and propagation

The bot must be connected before an interaction can be registered, unregistered, or synced. Global interactions can take up to an hour to appear in Discord. A guild interaction uses `guildId` to register in one server and normally appears immediately.

Changing `commandName`, `commandDescription`, or `options` changes Discord's command definition and requires registration again. Response actions, buttons, and cooldown behavior run locally and can be changed without re-registering the Discord command.

Sync All replaces the bot's registered global commands with the current interaction list, so removed interactions are also removed from Discord. The app manages `isRegistered`; it is a local status indicator, not confirmation from Discord on every launch.

records.title: Name and description
records.content:
## Name and description

`commandName` is the text users type after `/`. It must be 1-32 characters and follow Discord's slash-command naming rules; lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens, and underscores are the safest choices. Do not include the slash. `commandDescription` is required and must be 1-100 characters.

records.title: Command options
records.content:
## Command options

`options` are typed inputs Discord collects before running the command. Each option has a unique `name`, a `description`, a numeric `type`, and a `required` setting. Required options must be completed before Discord will submit the command.

Supported option types are 3 String, 4 Integer, 5 Boolean, 6 User, 7 Channel, 8 Role, and 10 Number. The optional `choices` array can restrict String, Integer, or Number options to named values when using JSON or agent editing.

records.title: Using option values
records.content:
## Using option values

Use `$option(name)` in any interaction response template, replacing `name` with the exact option name. User, Channel, and Role options produce the selected Discord ID, while other option types produce their submitted value.

```
Hello $option(person)! Your topic is $option(topic).
```

records.title: Messages and embeds
records.content:
## Messages and embeds

The root action can send a channel message, channel embed, private message, private embed, or any useful combination. Channel responses become the Discord interaction reply. Private responses are sent separately by direct message and can fail if the user blocks DMs.

Embed templates support a title, description, hex color, image URL, thumbnail URL, and footer. At least one visible embed field must be filled in. Interaction templates support the same BCFD keywords and functions as other commands when the required user, server, or channel context is available.

records.title: Ephemeral and deferred replies
records.content:
## Ephemeral and deferred replies

`ephemeral` makes the channel response visible only to the person who ran the command or clicked the button. It does not change separate direct messages.

`deferReply` acknowledges the interaction before longer template or Discord work finishes, avoiding Discord's short initial response deadline. The completed response keeps the configured ephemeral setting.

records.title: Assign or remove a role
records.content:
## Assign or remove a role

`isRoleAssigner` toggles `roleToAssign` on the member who ran the command or clicked the button. The bot needs Manage Roles, and its highest role must be above the target role. This action only works in a server.

records.title: Moderation actions
records.content:
## Moderation actions

`isKick`, `isBan`, and `isVoiceMute` target the User option named by `targetUserOptionName`. The caller and bot need the matching Discord permission. `deleteX` and `deleteNum` bulk-delete up to 100 messages and require Manage Messages in a text channel.

Moderation and bulk deletion are supported only on the interaction's root action. Button actions can send responses, send DMs, toggle roles, and show more buttons.

Moderation and bulk deletion are supported only on the interaction's root action. Button actions can send responses, send DMs, toggle roles, and show more buttons.

records.title: Button fields and styles
records.content:
## Button fields and styles

Each button has a `label`, numeric `style`, optional `emoji`, `disabled` state, and either a `customId` with an action or a link `url`. Styles are 1 Primary, 2 Secondary, 3 Success, 4 Danger, and 5 Link.

Every non-link button needs a unique `customId` so Bot Commander can find its action. Link buttons require a valid URL, open that URL directly, and do not run a Bot Commander action.

records.title: Button actions and nesting
records.content:
## Button actions and nesting

A non-link button has its own reusable interaction action. It can reply with text or an embed, send a DM, toggle a role, and include another set of buttons. Ephemeral and deferred reply settings apply separately to each button action.

Each response supports up to five buttons in one row. The editor allows three levels of nested button responses. Keep button labels and response paths short so users can understand where each click leads.

records.title: Inactive and outdated buttons
records.content:
## Inactive and outdated buttons

Buttons work only while the bot is hosted and the interaction still contains their custom IDs. If a button on an older Discord message can no longer be found, Bot Commander replies that the button is no longer active. Disabled buttons remain visible but cannot be clicked.

records.title: Cooldown settings
records.content:
## Cooldown settings

`cooldown` is the duration in seconds. Zero or an omitted value disables it. `cooldownType` can be User, Server, or Global to track usage per person, per server, or across every server.

`cooldownMessage` is an optional BCFD template returned ephemerally when a user is blocked. Use `$cooldownRemaining()` to include the remaining seconds. Without a custom message, Bot Commander sends its standard remaining-time response.

The cooldown applies when the slash command runs and is recorded after its root action succeeds. Button clicks do not start or check the command cooldown.

records.title: Interaction resource fields
records.content:
## Interaction resource fields

`commandName`, `commandDescription`, and `options` define the Discord slash command. `rootAction` defines its local response. `guildId` optionally limits registration to one server. `cooldown`, `cooldownType`, and `cooldownMessage` control rate limits.

`id` is assigned by the app and identifies the local resource. `isRegistered` is managed by the app. Preserve both fields when editing an existing interaction; new interactions should let the app assign them.

records.content:
## Interaction action fields

Response toggles and templates are `sendChannelMessage`, `channelMessage`, `sendPrivateMessage`, `privateMessage`, `sendChannelEmbed`, `channelEmbed`, `sendPrivateEmbed`, and `privateEmbed`.

Other fields are `isRoleAssigner`, `roleToAssign`, `isKick`, `isBan`, `isVoiceMute`, `targetUserOptionName`, `deleteX`, `deleteNum`, `ephemeral`, `deferReply`, and `buttons`.

records.title: Canonical interaction JSON example
records.content:
## Canonical interaction JSON example

```
{
  "id": "app-assigned",
  "commandName": "greet",
  "commandDescription": "Greet a member",
  "options": [
    { "name": "person", "description": "Who to greet", "type": 6, "required": true }
  ],
  "rootAction": {
    "sendChannelMessage": true,
    "channelMessage": "Hello $option(person)!",
    "sendPrivateMessage": false,
    "privateMessage": "",
    "sendChannelEmbed": false,
    "channelEmbed": { "title": "", "description": "", "hexColor": "", "imageURL": "", "thumbnailURL": "", "footer": "" },
    "sendPrivateEmbed": false,
    "privateEmbed": { "title": "", "description": "", "hexColor": "", "imageURL": "", "thumbnailURL": "", "footer": "" },
    "isRoleAssigner": false,
    "roleToAssign": "",
    "isKick": false,
    "isBan": false,
    "isVoiceMute": false,
    "targetUserOptionName": "",
    "deleteX": false,
    "deleteNum": 0,
    "ephemeral": false,
    "deferReply": false,
    "buttons": []
  },
  "isRegistered": false,
  "cooldown": 0,
  "cooldownType": "",
  "cooldownMessage": ""
}
```

Omit `guildId` for global registration or set it to one server ID for guild registration. Choices and nested buttons can be added to the corresponding arrays.

records.title: Discord Timestamp Keywords
records.content:
## Discord Timestamp Keywords

### Current BCFD interpreter; availability follows the corresponding base value

These variants return Discord timestamp markup so Discord renders a localized date and time.

`$timeCreatedDiscord` - caller account creation time.

`$memberTimeJoinedDiscord` - caller's server join time.

`$memberEffectiveTimeCreatedDiscord` - caller's underlying account creation time.

`$memberTimeBoostedDiscord` - time the caller began boosting.

`$botTimeCreatedDiscord` - bot account creation time.

`$serverCreateTimeDiscord` - server creation time.

`$channelCreateDateDiscord` - current channel creation time.

`$mentionedTimeCreatedDiscord` - mentioned user's account creation time.

`$dateDiscord` - current date and time.

If the required user, member, server, or channel context is unavailable, the result is empty.
records.searchableText:
Discord Timestamp Keywords
Keywords
Discord Timestamp Variants
## Discord Timestamp Keywords

### Current BCFD interpreter; availability follows the corresponding base value

These variants return Discord timestamp markup so Discord renders a localized date and time.

`$timeCreatedDiscord` - caller account creation time.

`$memberTimeJoinedDiscord` - caller's server join time.

`$memberEffectiveTimeCreatedDiscord` - caller's underlying account creation time.

`$memberTimeBoostedDiscord` - time the caller began boosting.

`$botTimeCreatedDiscord` - bot account creation time.

`$serverCreateTimeDiscord` - server creation time.

`$channelCreateDateDiscord` - current channel creation time.

`$mentionedTimeCreatedDiscord` - mentioned user's account creation time.

`$dateDiscord` - current date and time.

If the required user, member, server, or channel context is unavailable, the result is empty.

records.title: Mentioned Member Context
records.content:
## Mentioned Member Context

### Message Receive commands with a mentioned server member

These values describe the mentioned user's membership in the current server. They return an empty value when no mentioned member is available.

`$mentionedMemberIsOwner` - whether the member owns the server.

`$mentionedMemberEffectiveName`, `$mentionedMemberNickname`, and `$mentionedMemberID` - server display name, nickname, and member ID.

`$mentionedMemberHasTimeJoined`, `$mentionedMemberTimeJoined`, and `$mentionedMemberTimeJoinedDiscord` - join-time availability and values.

`$mentionedMemberEffectiveAvatar`, `$mentionedMemberEffectiveTag`, `$mentionedMemberEffectiveID`, `$mentionedMemberEffectiveTimeCreated`, `$mentionedMemberEffectiveTimeCreatedDiscord`, and `$mentionedMemberEffectiveDefaultAvatar` - underlying account information.

`$mentionedMemberTimeBoosted`, `$mentionedMemberTimeBoostedDiscord`, and `$mentionedMemberHasBoosted` - server boost information.

`$mentionedMemberColor`, `$mentionedMemberRoles`, and `$mentionedMemberRoleCount` - display color and role information.

records.title: Additional Keywords
records.content:
## Additional Keywords

### Current BCFD interpreter

`$memberEffectiveDefaultAvatar` - caller's underlying default avatar URL.

`$createPrivateChannel(name,type)` - creates a channel visible to the caller and bot. Type defaults to `text`; supported types are text, voice, category, announcement, stage, and forum. Requires Manage Channels and returns the new channel ID or a BCFD error.

`$hour`, `$minute`, and `$second` insert the current local time components, padded to two digits.

`$cooldownRemaining(level)` - remaining cooldown seconds. The optional level is `user`, `server`, or `global`; when omitted it uses the command's configured cooldown scope. Returns `0` outside a configured cooldown.

Keyword names are case-sensitive. Use `$ID` and `$defaultAvatar` with the exact Android-canonical spelling shown here. Older desktop spellings remain compatibility aliases.

records.title: Your First Command
records.content:
## Getting Started

Let's create your first bot command. We'll make a simple greeting that responds when someone types !hello in your server.

- Open Bot Commander and go to the Commands tab.

- Tap the + button to create a new command.

- Set the type to Message Received.

- Set the command name to !hello.

- In the channel message field, enter the response template shown below.

- Save the command, host your bot, and type !hello in a Discord channel to test it.

## Response Template

```
Hello $name! Welcome to $server.
```

## Discord Output

A

Alex

!hello

B

MyBot
BOT

Hello @Alex! Welcome to My Server.

records.title: Using Keywords
records.content:
## Variables & Functions

Keywords let you insert dynamic information into your bot's responses. Variables like $name and $server are replaced with real values, and functions like $rollnum() and $random{} generate content on the fly.

- Create a Message Received command named !info.

- Enter the response template below in the channel message field.

- Save, host your bot, and type !info to see the keywords in action.

## Response Template

```
Hey $name! Today is $date.
Server: $server | Members: $memberCount
Your random number: $rollnum(1,100)
Fortune: $random{Good luck today!|Take it easy|Big things are coming}
```

## Discord Output

A

Alex

!info

B

MyBot
BOT

Hey @Alex! Today is 3/12/2026, 2:30:00 PM.
Server: My Server | Members: 42
Your random number: 73
Fortune: Big things are coming

records.title: New vs Legacy Interpreter
records.content:
## Choosing a Template Interpreter

Bot Commander uses the newer BCFD interpreter by default. The
Use legacy interpreter setting switches back to the older
template processor for commands that depend on old behavior. Changing
this setting requires restarting the bot.

- Keep Use legacy interpreter turned off for new commands.

- Turn it on only if an older command stops working after an update.

- When updating old commands, test nested keywords, conditionals, and JavaScript blocks with the newer interpreter before removing the fallback.

## How They Differ

The legacy interpreter replaces keywords with a series of direct text
replacements. The newer interpreter parses the whole template first,
understands variables and functions as expressions, and evaluates nested
expressions from the inside out.

- Nested expressions: the newer interpreter can resolve templates like $sum($rollnum(1,10), $rollnum(1,10)). The legacy interpreter is best for simple, flat replacements.

- Conditional blocks: $if, $elseif, $else, and $endif are part of the newer interpreter.

- More functions: the newer interpreter includes math and string helpers such as $mul, $div, $round, $upper, $replace, $contains, and $cooldownRemaining.

- Escaping: use $$ when you want to send a literal dollar sign instead of starting a keyword.

- Error handling: newer interpreter errors are returned as [BCFD Error: ...] so the broken expression is easier to find.

## Legacy-Style Template

Simple variable and function replacements work in both interpreters and
are the safest pattern for older commands.

```
Hello $namePlain!
You said: $messageAfterCommand
Dice roll: $rollnum(1,20)
Mood: $random{great|focused|sleepy}
```

## New Interpreter Examples

These examples rely on the newer interpreter's parser, nested
evaluation, and conditional logic.

```
Total of two dice: $sum($rollnum(1,6), $rollnum(1,6))
Shout name: $upper($namePlain)

$if($argsCount > 0)
You asked about: $messageAfterCommand
$else
Ask a question after the command.
$endif
```

## JavaScript Eval Difference

In the newer interpreter, keywords inside $eval blocks are resolved
into JavaScript-safe temporary values before JavaScript runs, and the
block's return value becomes the message output. The legacy interpreter
runs the JavaScript block directly and removes it from the message, so
keywords must be placed inside your own string literals when you need them
as strings.

```
$eval
var userName = $namePlain;
return "Hello, " + userName + "!";
$halt
```

## Variable Scope and Startup JS

The newer interpreter runs each $eval block inside a temporary
function. Variables declared with var or let inside the
block are local to that one block, so later commands and later
$eval blocks cannot read them with $get(). The legacy
interpreter runs eval code directly in the shared JavaScript context, so
top-level variables from one block may remain available to later blocks.

```
$eval
var temporaryName = $namePlain;
$halt

After the block: $get(temporaryName)
```

With the newer interpreter, $get(temporaryName) will not see the
local variable from the eval block. With the legacy interpreter, direct
eval variables can leak into the shared VM context, which is useful for
some older commands but easier to break accidentally.

## When Startup JS Helps

Startup JS runs once when the JavaScript engine starts, before command
templates are processed. Use it to define helper functions or session-level
variables that many commands should share. For data that must survive bot
restarts, store it on botState instead.

```
// Startup JS
function formatGreeting(name) {
  return "Hello, " + name + "!";
}

var sessionUses = 0;
```

```
$eval
sessionUses++;
return formatGreeting($namePlain) + " Session uses: " + sessionUses;
$halt
```

This pattern works well with the newer interpreter because commands can
call shared startup helpers without leaking every temporary variable they
create while building a response.

## Benefits of the Newer Interpreter

- More predictable command output because templates are parsed before they are evaluated.

- Cleaner advanced commands with conditionals instead of putting all logic in JavaScript.

- Better composition because functions can be nested inside other functions.

- A larger built-in function set for math, text formatting, channel management, AI, cooldowns, and lookups.

- Clearer error messages when a template cannot be evaluated.

records.title: Slash Commands
records.content:
## Interaction Commands

Slash commands are Discord's modern way of interacting with bots. Users type / followed by the command name, and Discord shows autocomplete options. You can define typed parameters that Discord validates for you.

- Go to the Interactions tab and tap + to create a new interaction command.

- Set the command name to greet (no prefix needed for slash commands).

- Set the description to Greet someone.

- Add an option: type String, name person, description Who to greet.

- Set the response template shown below.

- Save, host your bot, and type /greet in Discord. Fill in the person option and send it.

## Response Template

```
Hello $option(person)! $name says hi!
```

## Discord Output

A

Alex

used /greet person: Sam

B

MyBot
BOT

Hello Sam! @Alex says hi!

records.title: Interactive Buttons
records.content:
## Buttons on Messages

You can add clickable buttons to your slash command responses. Each button can trigger its own response, assign roles, or show ephemeral messages that only the clicker can see.

- Create a new interaction command named menu with the description Show the menu.

- Set the response message to the template shown below.

- Add a button with label Info, style Primary, and ID info_btn. Set its response to Here is some info about our server!

- Add another button with label Help, style Secondary, and ID help_btn. Set its response to Need help? Ask in #support!

- Optionally, check Ephemeral on the button responses so only the person who clicks sees the reply.

- Save, host, and test with /menu in Discord.

## Response Template

```
Welcome to the menu! Choose an option below.
```

## Discord Output

A

Alex

used /menu

B

MyBot
BOT

Welcome to the menu! Choose an option below.

Info
Help

records.title: Conditional Responses
records.content:
## If / Else Logic

Use $if, $elseif, $else, and $endif to make your bot respond differently based on conditions. You can check arguments, user properties, server info, and more.

- Create a Message Received command named !role.

- Enter the conditional template below in the channel message field.

- Save and test. Try !role admin, !role mod, and !role anything to see the different responses.

## Response Template

```
$if($args(0) == admin)
You are an admin, $namePlain!
$elseif($args(0) == mod)
You are a moderator, $namePlain.
$else
You are a regular member, $namePlain.
$endif
```

## Discord Output

A

Alex

!role admin

B

MyBot
BOT

You are an admin, Alex!

## Alternate Output

A

Alex

!role hello

B

MyBot
BOT

You are a regular member, Alex.

records.title: Embed Messages
records.content:
## Rich Embeds

Embeds are rich, formatted messages with a colored side bar, title, description, footer, images, and thumbnails. They make your bot's responses look polished and professional.

- Create a Message Received command named !profile.

- Enable the Send Embed option.

- Fill in the embed fields as shown below. All fields are optional as long as you fill in at least one.

- Save, host, and type !profile to see your embed.

## Embed Fields

```
Title:       $namePlain's Profile
Description: Member of $server since the beginning!
Footer:      Requested on $date
Thumbnail:   $avatar
```

## Discord Output

A

Alex

!profile

B

MyBot
BOT

Alex's Profile

Member of My Server since the beginning!

Requested on 3/12/2026, 2:30:00 PM

records.title: Welcome Messages
records.content:
## Member Join Events

You can make your bot automatically greet new members when they join your server. Combine this with role assignment to give new members a default role.

- Create a new command and set the type to Member Join.

- Enter the response template below in the channel message field.

- Enable Assign/Remove Role and enter the role name (e.g. Member). This is case-sensitive.

- Save and host. When someone joins your server, the bot will greet them and assign the role automatically.

## Response Template

```
Welcome to $server, $name! You are member #$memberCount.
Please read the rules and enjoy your stay!
```

## Discord Output

B

MyBot
BOT

Welcome to My Server, @Alex! You are member #43.
Please read the rules and enjoy your stay!

records.title: Persisting State
records.content:
## Saving Data Between Sessions

The botState object lets you store data that persists even when you stop and restart the bot. You can modify it inside $eval...$halt blocks and read its properties with $get(). You can also use $set() and $get() outside of eval blocks to store and retrieve values on the VM context directly.

- Create a Message Received command named !count.

- Enter the template below. The $eval block runs JavaScript and its return value replaces the block in the message. The botState object persists across bot restarts.

- Save and test. The counter increments each time and survives bot restarts.

## Response Template

```
$eval
if (!botState.count) botState.count = 0;
botState.count++;
return "This command has been used " + botState.count + " times!";
$halt
```

## Discord Output

A

Alex

!count

B

MyBot
BOT

This command has been used 7 times!

records.title: Channel Management
records.content:
## Managing Channels

Bot Commander includes functions for creating, deleting, and managing Discord channels directly from your command responses. Your bot needs the Manage Channels permission for these to work.

- Create a Message Received command named !setup.

- Enter the template below. It creates a new text channel and lists all channels in the server.

- Save and test. Make sure your bot has the Manage Channels permission in your server.

## Response Template

```
$createChannel(welcome, text)
Channel created! Your server now has $channelCount channels: $listChannels
```

## Discord Output

A

Alex

!setup

B

MyBot
BOT

Channel created! Your server now has 4 channels: general, off-topic, voice-chat, welcome

records.title: AI-Powered Responses
records.content:
## Using AI in Commands

The $chat() function sends a prompt to AI and returns the response. Combine it with $messageAfterCommand to let users ask your bot anything. You'll need an API key configured in your bot settings.

- Create a Message Received command named !ask.

- Enter one of the response templates below. The simple version passes the user's question directly to AI. The advanced version adds formatting.

- Save and test. Type !ask followed by any question.

## Simple Template

```
$chat($messageAfterCommand)
```

## Advanced Template

```
$name asked: $messageAfterCommand

AI Response: $chat($messageAfterCommand)
```

## Discord Output

A

Alex

!ask What is the capital of France?

B

MyBot
BOT

@Alex asked: What is the capital of France?

AI Response: The capital of France is Paris!

records.content:
## Command Only, Starts With & Phrase

When you create a "Message Received" command, you can choose how the bot matches incoming messages. By default the message must exactly match your command name. The Starts With and Phrase options change this behavior.

## Command Only (Default)

The message must be exactly the command name, nothing more. For example, a command named !hello will only trigger when someone types exactly !hello. Typing !hello world or say !hello will not trigger it.

```
Command name: !hello
Triggers:     "!hello"        ✓
              "!hello world"  ✗
              "say !hello"    ✗
```

## Starts With

Enable Starts With to trigger the command when a message begins with the command name. This is useful for commands that accept arguments. Use $args(0), $args(1), etc. to access the words after the command, or $messageAfterCommand to get everything after the command name.

```
Command name: !say
Starts With:  ✓
Triggers:     "!say hello"          ✓
              "!say how are you"    ✓
              "!say"                ✓
              "I say hello"         ✗
```

A

Alex

!say good morning everyone

B

MyBot
BOT

Alex says: good morning everyone

## Phrase

Enable Phrase to trigger the command whenever the command name appears anywhere in a message. The match is case-insensitive. This is great for auto-responses to certain words or topics.

```
Command name: dog
Phrase:       ✓
Triggers:     "I love my dog"       ✓
              "DOG is the best"     ✓
              "hotdog"              ✓
              "I love my cat"       ✗
```

A

Alex

I love my dog so much

B

MyBot
BOT

Dogs are the best! 🐕

records.title: Moderation Commands
records.content:
## Kick, Ban & Voice Mute

Bot Commander has built-in moderation actions. When you enable kick, ban, or voice mute on a command, the bot performs that action on the mentioned user. The user running the command must have the matching Discord permission (Kick Members, Ban Members, or Mute Members). Usage is always: command @user.

## Kick Command

Creates a command that kicks the mentioned user from the server.

- Create a Message Received command named !kick.

- Enable the Kick option in the moderation section.

- Optionally add a channel message like the template below to confirm the action.

- Save and test with !kick @user. Both you and the bot need the Kick Members permission.

```
$mentionedNamePlain has been kicked from the server.
```

A

Alex

!kick @Troublemaker

B

MyBot
BOT

Troublemaker has been kicked from the server.

## Ban Command

Works the same way as kick, but permanently bans the user from the server.

- Create a Message Received command named !ban.

- Enable the Ban option in the moderation section.

- Save and test with !ban @user. Both you and the bot need the Ban Members permission.

```
$mentionedNamePlain has been banned. Goodbye!
```

## Voice Mute Command

Toggles server mute on a user in a voice channel. If they are unmuted, the bot mutes them. If they are already muted, it unmutes them.

- Create a Message Received command named !mute.

- Enable the Voice Mute option in the moderation section.

- Save and test with !mute @user. You need the Mute Members permission and the target must be in a voice channel.

records.title: Role Assignment
records.content:
## Assigning & Removing Roles

The Assign/Remove Role option toggles a role on the user who triggers the command. If they don't have the role, it's added. If they already have it, it's removed. This works with message commands, interaction buttons, and event-based commands like Member Join.

## Toggle Role Command

A command that lets users give themselves (or remove) a role.

- Create a Message Received command named !color-red.

- Enable Assign/Remove Role and enter the role name or ID in the Role field (e.g. Red). This is case-sensitive.

- Add a channel message to confirm the action.

- Save and test. The bot needs the Manage Roles permission and its role must be higher than the role it's assigning.

```
$if($memberNickname == Red)
Removed the Red role from you, $namePlain.
$else
You now have the Red role, $namePlain!
$endif
```

A

Alex

!color-red

B

MyBot
BOT

You now have the Red role, Alex!

## Auto-Role on Join

You can also assign a role automatically when someone joins your server. Create a Member Join command with Assign/Remove Role enabled. Since a joining member never has the role yet, it will always be added. See the Welcome Messages tutorial for the full setup.

## Role via Buttons

For a more modern approach, use an interaction command with buttons. Each button can assign a different role when clicked. Enable Assign/Remove Role on each button's action and set the corresponding role name. See the Interactive Buttons tutorial for how to set up buttons.

records.title: Reactions
records.content:
## Adding & Responding to Reactions

There are two ways to use reactions in Bot Commander: making your bot add a reaction to a message as part of a command response, and creating commands that trigger when a user reacts to a message.

## Bot Adds a Reaction

Any command can make the bot react to the triggering message. This is useful for acknowledgment or fun responses.

- Create a Message Received command (e.g. !thumbs).

- Enable the React option and enter the emoji name. For default emojis, use the emoji directly. For custom server emojis, use the emoji name without colons.

- Save and test. The bot will add the reaction to the message that triggered the command.

## Reaction Roles

A popular feature that lets users react to a specific message to gain or remove a role. This uses the Reaction Add command type.

- Create a command and set the type to Reaction Add.

- Set the command name to the emoji that will trigger the role. For default emojis use the emoji itself (e.g. ⭐). For custom emojis, use the name without colons.

- Enable Require Specific Message and paste the message ID of the message users will react to. To get a message ID, enable Developer Mode in Discord settings, then right-click the message and select Copy Message ID.

- Enable Assign/Remove Role and enter the role name (e.g. Announcements).

- Save and host. React to the message with the emoji to gain the role, react again to remove it.

- Repeat for each role you want to assign. Create one command per emoji/role combination.

```
Example setup for a role selection message:

⭐ → Announcements role
🎮 → Gamer role
🎨 → Artist role

Each emoji gets its own Reaction Add command
with the matching role name.
```

records.title: Using JavaScript
Using Webhooks
records.content:
What can you do?

These allow you to send messages to a certain channel using the built-in webhooks integration on Discord. You can send either a regular message or an embed.

How do I create a webhook for my server?

Go to your desired server and click on "Server Settings", from there you click on "Integrations" and then "Create Webhook". This will create the webhook for you and all you need to do is copy the webhook URL to the Bot Commander app.
